raw_case:
Dural arteriovenous fistula (DAVF) after venous sinus stent (VSS) for presumed idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH): A case report and literature review
Dural arteriovenous fistulas (DAVFs) are rare but significant intracranial vascular malformations that are usually idiopathic and can lead to severe complications like venous hypertension and intracranial hemorrhage. We present 2 cases of DAVF occurring after venous sinus stenting (VSS) for presumed idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH) in a 51-year-old and a 52-year-old female. In both patients, Cognard type 1 DAVF was detected by Catheter angiography and successfully obliterated with Onyx™ embolization. These cases suggest that VSS may either unmask pre-existing DAVFs by altering intracranial pressure dynamics or induce new fistula formation by angiogenic factors or stent-induced hemodynamic changes. Clinicians should maintain an index of suspicion for DAVF in patients with persistent symptoms post-VSS and catheter angiography might be needed to detect the DAVFs.

Introduction

Dural arteriovenous fistulas (DAVFs) represent 10-15% of intracranial vascular malformations and are abnormal shunts between the meningeal arteries and the intracranial venous system [1]. Patients with DAVFs often present with tinnitus, headache, and dizziness. DAVFs can potentially cause serious complications such as venous hypertension and intracranial hemorrhage if retrograde cortical venous drainage is present. The exact etiology of DAVFs is unclear; although causes include trauma, inflammation, or sinus thrombosis, most cases are considered idiopathic [2]. We present 2 unusual cases of DAVF following venous sinus stenting (VSS) for presumed idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH) and review the English language literature on this rare complication.

Case report

A 52-year-old white female presented with increasing headaches and blurred vision. Her past medical history was notable for migraines and hypertension. She had rheumatoid arthritis and a prior deep vein thrombosis (DVT) with a pulmonary embolism (PE) and nephrolithiasis with secondary hydronephrosis. She was a nonsmoker with a body mass index (BMI) of 31. Her medications included fremanezumab, ubrogepant, hydrochlorothiazide, valsartan, clonidine, rivaroxaban, tizanidine, clonazepam, methotrexate, promethazine, and pramipexole.A neuro-ophthalmic exam showed visual acuity of 20/25 in both eyes (OU). Pupils were isocoric without relative afferent pupillary defect. Color testing with Ishihara color plates was 3/14 in the right eye (OD) and 1/14 in the left eye (OS). Dilated ophthalmoscopy showed Frisén grade 2 optic disc edema OU. Automated perimetry (Humphrey Visual Field [HVF] 24-2 testing) was normal OU. Optical coherence tomography (OCT) of the peripapillary retinal nerve fiber layer (RNFL) revealed a global thickness of 79 µm OD and 77 µm OS. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the brain and orbit with and without contrast demonstrated flattening of the globes, partial empty sella, and stenosis but no thrombosis of the distal left transverse sinus. MR venography (MRV) confirmed the narrowing of the left transverse and sigmoid sinuses. Lumbar puncture opening pressure was 34 cm H2O, with unremarkable cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis. Catheter angiogram and venous sinus manometry showed a pressure gradient of 5 mm Hg across the stenosis without evidence of a DAVF or AV malformation.The patient was diagnosed with IIH by modified Dandy criteria and started on acetazolamide and topiramate. Due to worsening symptoms despite maximal medical therapy, she underwent venous sinus stenting (VSS) on the left transverse sinus and proximal sigmoid sinus (Fig. 1) using both a Nickel-Titanium (Precise™) and a cobalt-chromium alloy (Wallstent™) stent 4 months after her initial presentation. At that time, an external carotid angiogram was negative for DAVF. Four months after VSS, the patient reported new left-sided headaches, memory loss, tinnitus, and hearing loss described as “people talking underwater”. Ophthalmologic examination revealed no disc swelling, normal HVF, and stable OCT. Computed tomography (CT) and MRI of the head were unremarkable. Cerebral Angiogram revealed a Cognard type 1 left DAVF. Liquid (Onyx™) embolization obliterated the DAVF successfully, maintaining patent antegrade flow through bilateral transverse sinuses (Fig. 2).The second case was a 51-year-old white female, with a medical history significant for migraine, depression, and prediabetes, and no significant family or surgical history. She presented with a prolonged history of right-sided whooshing tinnitus. A cerebral angiogram revealed stenosis at the right transverse-sigmoid sinus junction with an 8 mmHg pressure gradient, alongside a high-riding jugular bulb. Endovascular treatment was planned, leading to the successful placement of 2 wall stents at the right transverse-sigmoid sinus junction, with complete resolution of the stenosis (Fig. 3).Four months later, she presented to the emergency department with a week-long intermittent right-sided headache, radiating to the back of the head, and not relieved by over-the-counter painkillers. A follow-up cerebral angiogram showed the development of type 1 Cognard classification right transverse-sigmoid sinus dural fistula, primarily supplied by the right middle meningeal artery and right occipital artery, with no reflux into the cortical venous system. Liquid (Onyx™) embolization resulted in full resolution of the fistula (Fig. 4).

Discussion

DAVFs have been documented with venous congestion, infection, tumors, hormonal imbalance, hypercoagulable states, and arterial dysplasia. These cranial pathologies contribute to chronic hypoperfusion and dural hypoxia which stimulate angiogenesis. The cardiac literature has documented increased vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) and platelet-derived growth factor (PDGF) after coronary artery stents [3]. Although increased VEGF and PDGF have been documented in the local tissue following VSS [2], the degree of increase is unclear. We propose that a similar angiogenic mechanism could promote DAVF pathogenesis after VSS. Other proposed mechanisms suggest that the stent (foreign body) may have induced transient cortical vein occlusion and subsequent DAVF formation [4], while another case of DAVF after a carotid artery–cavernous sinus stent attributed stent-dependent hemodynamic alterations to have promoted fistula revascularization via small branches of the meningeal supply [5]. In this case, we hypothesize that the increased ICP could have been acting as a tamponade, and paradoxically lowering the ICP may have contributed to the unmasking of the DAVF.Table 1 summarizes 4 cases of DAVF after stenting. The patients, all females aged 22 to 51 years old, developed DAVFs 3 to 4 months after stenting. Common symptoms included progressive tinnitus, headaches, and visual disturbances. Branches of the external carotid artery typically fed the DAVFs. Management strategies varied, with most cases treated via embolization, although one case was managed conservatively.Our cases raise the question of whether VSS and the resulting altered venous flow could have unmasked the pre-existing DAVF in this patient. In such cases, presumably elevated intracranial pressure acts to tamponade the DAVF. Alternatively, VSS could have triggered the release of angiogenic factors contributing to the development of a de novo DAVF. Clinicians should maintain an index of suspicion for DAVF in patients with persistent or worsening symptoms following VSS. Catheter angiography may be necessary to document the DAVF, confirm retrograde or anterograde flow, and the presence or absence of cortical venous drainage.The presence of higher-grade DAVF, symptomatic cerebral venous hypertension, or retrograde high-flow cortical venous drainage may be indications for endovascular embolization to prevent potentially life-threatening complications. Radiosurgery is typically used for low-grade (type I Borden) DAVFs with persistent symptoms, such as pulsatile tinnitus in our 2 cases. It can also be considered for DAVFs with patients with significant medical comorbidities, in cases where the anatomy is unsuitable for other interventions, or as a salvage treatment for lesions that remain incompletely treated after endovascular embolization [9,10].

Authorship

All authors attest that they meet the current ICMJE criteria for Authorship.

Patient consent

This report does not contain any personal identifying information and consent was obtained from the patients.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information**: Two female patients (51 and 52 years old) who presented with distinct symptoms.
- **Chief Complaint**: 
  - Case 1: Progressive headaches and blurred vision.
  - Case 2: Prolonged right-sided pulsatile tinnitus.
- **History of Present Illness**:
  - Case 1: Symptoms worsened despite medical therapy for IIH; developed new left-sided headaches, tinnitus, and hearing changes post-VSS.
  - Case 2: Developed intermittent right-sided headaches radiating to the back of the head a week prior, after initial resolution of symptoms post-VSS.
- **Past Medical History**:
  - Case 1: Migraines, hypertension, rheumatoid arthritis, prior DVT and PE, nephrolithiasis.
  - Case 2: Migraines, depression, prediabetes.
- **Personal History**:
  - Case 1: Nonsmoker; BMI of 31.
  - Case 2: No significant personal history.
- **Family History**: None stated for either case.
- **Physical Examination**:
  - Case 1: Neuro-ophthalmic exam revealed optic disc edema (Frisén grade 2); OCT findings consistent with papilledema.
  - Case 2: Not specifically detailed in the report.
- **Ancillary Tests**:
  - Case 1: MRI/MRV showed left transverse sinus stenosis without thrombosis. Elevated CSF opening pressure (34 cm H2O). Post-VSS angiogram displayed a Cognard type 1 DAVF successfully obliterated with Onyx™ embolization.
  - Case 2: Initial angiogram evidenced right transverse-sigmoid sinus stenosis with a pressure gradient. Follow-up angiogram revealed a Cognard type 1 DAVF that was successfully treated with Onyx™ embolization.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Idiopathic Intracranial Hypertension (IIH)**:
   - Included initially due to symptoms consistent with diagnostic criteria for IIH (e.g., elevated opening pressure, optic disc edema, MRI findings typical of IIH).
   - Supportive findings: No thrombosis; symptoms initially responded to medical therapy.

2. **Post-VSS complications (DAVF)**:
   - Suspected based on new or persistent symptoms after VSS, such as tinnitus, headaches, and hearing changes.
   - Confirmed by catheter angiography demonstrating Cognard type 1 DAVFs in both cases. Mechanisms considered include angiogenic effects of altered hemodynamics, possible unmasking of pre-existing DAVF by normalization of intracranial pressure, or stent-related factors (e.g., local angiogenic activity, turbulence-induced changes).

3. **Vascular Malformations or Sinus Pathologies**:
   - Ruled out on initial workup (e.g., angiogram before VSS negative for DAVF or arteriovenous malformation).
   - Development of DAVF after VSS suggests secondary etiology rather than a primary pre-existing vascular anomaly.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Cognard type 1 dural arteriovenous fistula (DAVF) in both cases, likely secondary to venous sinus stenting (VSS) performed for presumed idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH).

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's start by looking at both cases. Both patients are middle-aged women with similar ages. They both presented with different initial symptoms but ended up having VSS (venous sinus stenting) and later developed DAVFs (dural arteriovenous fistulas). 

For Case 1, the main complaints were progressive headaches and blurred vision. She had a history of IIH (idiopathic intracranial hypertension), which is often associated with conditions like obesity (her BMI is 31, which is obese). Her MRI/MRV showed left transverse sinus stenosis without thrombosis. The elevated CSF opening pressure of 34 cm H2O (normal is up to 25) confirms IIH. After VSS, she developed new symptoms like left-sided headaches and tinnitus. The angiogram post-VSS showed a Cognard type 1 DAVF treated with embolization. So, the initial treatment for IIH with VSS might have led to the development of DAVF as a complication.

Case 2 had pulsatile tinnitus, which is a common symptom of DAVF. Her initial treatment was also VSS for right transverse-sigmoid sinus stenosis with a pressure gradient. After VSS, she had headaches that resolved but then came back. Follow-up angiogram showed a Cognard type 1 DAVF, which was treated. 

Both cases developed DAVF after VSS. DAVFs are abnormal connections between arteries and veins in the dura mater. Cognard type 1 is the least aggressive, with antegrade venous drainage and low risk of hemorrhage. The key point here is that venous sinus stenting can sometimes lead to the formation of DAVFs. The mechanism might be related to altered hemodynamics in the venous sinuses after stenting, causing increased pressure or turbulence that leads to the development of fistulas. 

The patients’ histories of migraines and other conditions (like hypertension, rheumatoid arthritis in Case 1) might be comorbidities but not directly related to the DAVF formation. The timing of DAVF occurrence post-VSS is crucial. Both had successful embolization, so the final diagnosis for both would be DAVF, specifically Cognard type 1. But since the question is asking for the diagnosis result for each case, considering their presentations and the procedures, the main diagnosis after the VSS complication is DAVF.
results.content:
```
### Resoning:
<step 1> Both patients underwent venous sinus stenting (VSS) to address transverse sinus stenosis, which is commonly performed for idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH) or pulsatile tinnitus.  
<step 2> Post-VSS, both developed new or recurrent symptoms (headaches, tinnitus, hearing changes) and subsequent angiograms revealed Cognard type 1 dural arteriovenous fistulas (DAVFs).  
<step 3> DAVFs are abnormal arteriovenous shunts within the dura, and Cognard type 1 denotes low-risk lesions with antegrade venous drainage.  
<step 4> The temporal association of DAVF development after VSS suggests a procedural complication, possibly due to altered venous hemodynamics or endothelial injury during stent placement.  
<step 5> Both cases required definitive treatment with Onyx™ embolization, confirming the DAVF as the unifying diagnosis for their post-stent symptomatic progression.  

### Answer:
Case 1: Cognard type 1 dural arteriovenous fistula (DAVF) post-venous sinus stenting.  
Case 2: Cognard type 1 dural arteriovenous fistula (DAVF) post-venous sinus stenting.

reasoning_eval.step: Both patients underwent venous sinus stenting (VSS) to address transverse sinus stenosis, which is commonly performed for idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH) or pulsatile tinnitus.
Post-VSS, both developed new or recurrent symptoms (headaches, tinnitus, hearing changes) and subsequent angiograms revealed Cognard type 1 dural arteriovenous fistulas (DAVFs).
DAVFs are abnormal arteriovenous shunts within the dura, and Cognard type 1 denotes low-risk lesions with antegrade venous drainage.
The temporal association of DAVF development after VSS suggests a procedural complication, possibly due to altered venous hemodynamics or endothelial injury during stent placement.
Both cases required definitive treatment with Onyx™ embolization, confirming the DAVF as the unifying diagnosis for their post-stent symptomatic progression.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Step 1: Idiopathic Intracranial Hypertension (IIH) was initially included due to symptoms consistent with its diagnostic criteria, including elevated opening pressure, optic disc edema, and typical MRI findings. Supportive findings included no thrombosis and an initial response to medical therapy.
Step 2: Post-VSS complications were suspected based on new or persistent symptoms after VSS, such as tinnitus, headaches, and hearing changes.

Step 3: Cognard type 1 DAVFs were confirmed in both cases via catheter angiography, indicating mechanisms like angiogenic effects of altered hemodynamics, possible unmasking of pre-existing DAVF by normalized intracranial pressure, or stent-related factors such as local angiogenic activity or turbulence-induced changes.
Step 4: Vascular malformations or sinus pathologies were ruled out on initial workup, as angiograms before VSS were negative for DAVF or arteriovenous malformation.
Step 5: The development of DAVF after VSS suggests a secondary etiology rather than a primary pre-existing vascular anomaly.

Step 6: Final diagnosis: Cognard type 1 dural arteriovenous fistula (DAVF) in both cases, likely secondary to venous sinus stenting (VSS) performed for presumed idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH).
